cases.title: House Fire with Airway Risk
cases.presentation: A man was pulled from a burning bedroom. He has facial burns, singed nasal hairs, soot around his mouth, and a hoarse voice. He is coughing up black sputum.
cases.question: What is the priority intervention?
options: Apply a dry dressing to the face and reassess in 20 minutes
Anticipate airway swelling—give high-flow oxygen and secure the airway early; treat for possible CO poisoning
Trust the 98% SpO2 and provide only routine care
Cool the face with prolonged wet dressings
cases.explanation: Facial burns, singed hairs, soot, hoarseness, and carbonaceous sputum signal impending airway obstruction from inhalation injury. Give high-flow oxygen and secure the airway early before edema closes it. The 98% SpO2 is falsely reassuring—carbon monoxide poisoning must be treated empirically with 100% oxygen.
cases.clinicalPearl: In inhalation injury, the window for an easy airway closes fast—intubate early and never trust the pulse oximeter for CO.

cases.title: Estimating Burn Size
cases.presentation: An adult has partial- and full-thickness burns covering the entire front of the torso, the entire left arm, and the entire anterior aspect of one leg.
cases.question: Using the adult rule of nines, approximately what TBSA is burned?
cases.explanation: Anterior trunk = 18%, entire left arm = 9%, anterior of one leg = 9% (half of 18%), totaling approximately 36% TBSA. Only partial- and full-thickness burns are counted. This large burn warrants fluid resuscitation per protocol and burn-center transport.
cases.clinicalPearl: Memorize the rule of nines cold—accurate TBSA drives fluid resuscitation and triage decisions.

cases.title: Electrical Injury
cases.presentation: A lineman contacted a high-voltage wire. He has a small charred entry wound on one hand and an exit wound on his foot, and he is now having palpitations. The scene has been confirmed safe by the power company.
cases.question: What is the key concern despite small-appearing skin wounds?
options: The injury is minor because the skin wounds are small
Extensive deep tissue injury along the current path and cardiac dysrhythmias—apply cardiac monitoring and transport
cases.explanation: Electrical injury causes deep tissue destruction along the current's path far beyond the visible entry/exit wounds, plus a high risk of dysrhythmias and cardiac arrest. Apply cardiac monitoring, treat dysrhythmias, anticipate rhabdomyolysis, and transport—ideally to a burn center.
cases.clinicalPearl: In electrical injury, the skin lies—deep tissue and the heart bear the real damage. Always monitor the rhythm.
